name: Problems on Trains — Length, Speed and Crossing
whatIsIt: Trains are speed-distance-time problems with one twist: a train has LENGTH, so the distance it must cover to 'cross' something includes its own length. To pass a pole (a point) it travels its own length; to pass a platform or another train it travels the SUM of the two lengths. When two trains meet, use RELATIVE speed: add speeds if they move opposite ways, subtract if same way. Convert km/h to m/s by multiplying by 5/18.
whyNeeded: Trains is one of the most asked aptitude topics in SSC, Bank and Railway exams. The same relative-speed idea explains overtaking on a highway, two people walking towards each other, and any 'closing the gap' problem.
realLifeUses: A train passing a signal pole or a person standing still
A train crossing a bridge or platform
Two trains crossing each other on parallel tracks
One car overtaking another on a highway (same-direction relative speed)
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: The train covers just its OWN length; time = length ÷ speed.
The train covers train length + platform length.
Opposite directions → ADD speeds; same direction → SUBTRACT speeds.
Multiply by 5/18 (and m/s → km/h multiply by 18/5).

story.title: How Long to Pass the Platform?
story.text: Imran watches a 200-metre train rush past the 300-metre platform. 'How long does it take?' The station master explains: 'The train's front enters and its tail must leave — so it covers train PLUS platform = 200 + 300 = 500 metres. Its speed is 36 km/h, which is 36 × 5/18 = 10 metres per second. Time = 500 ÷ 10 = 50 seconds.' Then a pole: 'Past a pole it only covers its own 200 m → 200 ÷ 10 = 20 seconds.' Imran finally sees why the platform takes longer.
story.extractedProblem: A 200 m train at 36 km/h crosses a 300 m platform. How many seconds?

bars.label: Cross a POLE: distance = train length only

visual.caption: A train's crossing distance includes its own length. Pole = length; platform/train = sum of lengths. Convert km/h to m/s with ×5/18.

teachingGallery.title: Distance includes the train's length
teachingGallery.note: Pole → length. Platform/other train → sum of lengths. Two trains: add speeds (opposite) or subtract (same way).

standardMethod.summary: Convert speed to m/s; find the total distance (include lengths); time = distance ÷ (relative) speed.
steps: Distance: pole = train length; platform/train = sum of the two lengths.
For two trains, relative speed = sum (opposite directions) or difference (same direction).

alternateMethods.name: Keep km & hours
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When lengths are in km and speed in km/h (no conversion needed).
alternateMethods.example: A 0.5 km train at 30 km/h past a pole → 0.5 ÷ 30 h = 1 minute.

workedExamples.problem: A 180 m train runs at 54 km/h. How many seconds to pass a pole?
steps: Distance past a pole = train length = 180 m.

workedExamples.problem: Two trains, 100 m and 150 m long, move toward each other at 10 m/s and 15 m/s. How many seconds to cross each other?

trickPractice.trick: ×5/18 for m/s; add lengths, add/subtract speeds
trickPractice.intro: Convert first, then include lengths and pick the right relative speed.

questions.q: 120 m train at 20 m/s past a pole — seconds?
Two trains same direction, 20 m/s and 12 m/s. Relative speed (m/s)?
200 m train + 100 m platform at 30 m/s — seconds?

commonMistakes.mistake: Using only the platform length (or only the speed) and forgetting to add the train's own length.
commonMistakes.fix: A train isn't a point. To fully cross something, its tail must clear it — so add the train's length. Pole is the only case where distance equals just the train length.

commonMistakes.mistake: Subtracting speeds for trains moving toward each other, or adding for same-direction.
commonMistakes.fix: Toward each other (opposite) → ADD speeds (they close fast). Same direction → SUBTRACT (the gap closes slowly).

easy.q: To cross a platform, a train travels a distance equal to…

medium.q: 150 m train at 15 m/s past a pole — seconds?
Two trains toward each other at 18 m/s and 12 m/s. Relative speed (m/s)?

challenge.q: A 250 m train at 25 m/s crosses a 250 m bridge. Seconds?
Two trains run in the SAME direction. Their relative speed is…

questions.q: 100 m train at 10 m/s past a pole — seconds?
Passing a pole, a train covers a distance equal to…
Two trains toward each other, total length 300 m, relative speed 30 m/s. Seconds to cross?
180 m train + 120 m platform at 20 m/s — seconds?

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend the train rule: pole = own length, platform = train + platform, opposite trains add speeds, same-direction subtract. Use a 200 m train at 36 km/h past a 300 m platform and show the 50-second answer.

revisionCard.summary: km/h → m/s: ×5/18. Pole: distance = train length. Platform/other train: sum of lengths. Two trains: relative speed = sum (opposite) or difference (same way). Time = distance ÷ speed. Always include the train's own length.

gameMission.title: Clear the Platform
gameMission.brief: Time every crossing! Convert to m/s, add the lengths, choose add-or-subtract for two trains, and beat the buzzer — without forgetting the train's own length.

realLifeProject: Watch (or imagine) a train pass a platform. Estimate the train length and speed, convert km/h to m/s, and predict the crossing time — then time it and compare.

rememberIt.hook: Include the train's length; ×5/18 for m/s
rememberIt.unpack: Pole = length. Platform = sum. Opposite add, same subtract. Time = distance ÷ speed.

formulas.name: Crossing time
formulas.remember: sum of lengths over relative speed

studentQuestions.q: Why does the platform take longer than the pole?
studentQuestions.a: Because the train must cover its own length PLUS the platform's length to fully clear it, not just its own length.

studentQuestions.q: When do speeds add and when do they subtract?
studentQuestions.a: Add when the trains move toward each other (opposite directions); subtract when they move the same way.
